key_insight: A machine-learning interatomic potential reveals that Mg transmutation products and intrinsic defects in 3C-SiC reduce thermal conductivity in strongly configuration-dependent ways, with V_C and Mg_TC scattering phonons most strongly.
engineering_relevance: The results provide a quantitative atomistic basis for predicting and managing radiation- and transmutation-induced heat-transport degradation in nuclear-grade SiC ceramics.
method: Trained MLIP4SiC-Mg on a large DFT dataset for intrinsic defects, Mg defects, and Mg-defect complexes in 3C-SiC.
Combined Green-Kubo molecular dynamics with force-error correction to compute thermal conductivity in large defective supercells.
Used a resistance-based framework to treat dilute defects and compare defect configurations via residual thermal resistivity.
evidence_signal: The model reproduces DFT energies, forces, equation-of-state behavior, phonon dispersions, and lattice thermal conductivities, and predicts corrected pristine 3C-SiC thermal conductivity of 421 W/(mK) at 300 K, consistent with experiment.
watch_item: Residual thermal resistance is not strictly linear with defect concentration, so defect effects must be interpreted as effective temperature- and concentration-dependent scattering rather than simple additive contributions.
paper_title: Thermal Transport in SiC with Intrinsic Defects and Mg Transmutation Products
